# Refusal directions, harm schemas, evasion and defense of probe/latent monitors

> Living tensor document. We decompose the problem along several axes and look at which cells are
> covered / partially covered / not covered. Each cell is populated with papers carrying tags.
> Goal — to see the empty cells = where to dig, and to estimate cost.
>
> **What this map is for:** to surface, in RED wherever possible, the field's questions, sub-questions
> and details still UNANSWERED — and for every question to show whether it is asked, still askable,
> answered (and how well), or open and needing research. Three surfaces carry the questions: per-cell
> aspects → the `open-questions` status, future-work questions → the `answeredness` overlay, and red
> `required-uncovered` cells.
>
> Last updated: 2026-06-17 · papers in catalog: ~155 · future-work actually read: ~142/155 · the future-work graph (paper→cell edges) is moved out to `docs/FIELD_MAP_edges_v2.en.md`
>
> Access to papers — **direct `[pdf]` links in the §2 catalog** (read in browser, do not download).
> All arXiv-ids are **verified page-by-page** (arxiv.org/abs) — correct; titles/authors clarified.
> The map spans N equal tensor axes (RQ, MECH, MODALITY, ACCESS, STAGE, LOCUS, BEHAVIOR, POLARITY
> and the dynamics/architecture/non-unique-direction axes — no privileged pair); along most of the
> axes added in revision 8 the cells are empty or barely touched (see §4-bis).
> The catalog is grouped into named thematic clusters (a cluster = a group of catalog papers that share one research thread, e.g. "Attacks: GCG and successors"); a thinly-covered research question may still be marked ⚗️.
>
> NB: transfer of the refusal direction across families is **CONTESTED**: some works show transfer
> (P66 Trajectory Replay, P67 cross-lingual), others — lab-specific/non-transferable (P69/P79).

---

## 0. Tensor axes and legend

We view the problem along axes:

- **RQ axis (research question)** — RQ1…RQ13, see §3.
- **MECH axis (method/lens)** — what instrument is used to look:
  - `behavior` (ASR, refusal-rate); `directions` linear directions/steering; `sparse-autoencoders`;
  - `neurons` neurons/circuit (MLP/attention); `attention` attention flow; `training` training/finetune.
- **statuses: `coverage`, `score` and `open-questions` (graded facts of the CELL, not axes you author on papers)** — each tensor point carries three statuses the map computes itself and the site can color by, all distinct from the axes and all DERIVED from the SAME authored aspect checklist (three readings of one grade, not three authored facts). `coverage` is the discrete tier: either `required-uncovered` (red: no paper sits there yet) or one of three covered depths `thinly-covered`, `partially-covered`, `well-covered` (red → burnt-orange → amber → green). `score` is that same depth as a continuous 0..1 fraction (closed/total aspects), drawn as a smooth gradient over the same gamut; a `required-uncovered` gap has no score and stays red in either mode. `open-questions` is that same checklist read as a COUNT of still-unanswered sub-questions: an authored gap stays red, then a covered point ramps `3 open` / `2 open` / `1 open` down to `answered` (0 open) — so a point that looks filled but is barely answered reads as alarming as a near-gap (in an aggregate the most-open cell wins). There is no manual STATUS axis and no 6-tier judgement. Crucially, all three are computed by the map alone and **never read from the edges**:
  - **covered (graded by depth)** — DERIVED: a cell is a POINT of the tensor (a full coordinate tuple) made by grouping the catalog papers on their complete coordinates; any point a paper occupies is covered. Its depth tier comes from an authored **aspect checklist** in the `fieldmap-aspects` block: `score = closed / total aspects`, bucketed `<1/3` thinly, `<2/3` partially, else well. The aspects are SUBSTANTIVE and per-cell — two corpus-maturity rungs (*independently corroborated by a second study*; *robustly established by three or more*) plus, where the field asks for work here, whether that converging future-work demand is resolved by a paper in this cell — and each not-yet-complete covered cell carries a note ('what would deepen this point') listing the missing corroboration and the field's still-open questions. Most points hold a single unreplicated paper, so the field is mostly thin; the few deep points (e.g. `direction × directions`) stand out green.
  - **required-uncovered** — AUTHORED in the `fieldmap-uncovered` block: an empty but coverable tensor point a researcher could fill with a paper. Each row is the FULL coordinate tuple (a value on every axis, like a covered cell - the spec of the paper that would cover it) + a note on the experiment. No axis pair is privileged; a gap is a point like any other. This is the research gap you see red on the map; it must hold zero papers and carries no aspect checklist.
- **Future-work edges are a separate view.** `FIELD_MAP_edges.md` independently lists what the field's future-work asks for (`requiredUncoveredCells`, `partiallyCoveredCells`), shown only in the future-work graph. That demand never feeds the map's `coverage`.
- **CODE axis** — `released` public code exists / `partial` partial/dataset / `not-released` none.
- **MODELS axis** — size/families; `multi` = many families.
- **TRANSFER axis** — whether cross-model transfer was checked: `yes` / `no` / `partial` / `not-tested`.

**Axes added in revision 8 (previously the tensor was "collapsed" along them — almost everything sat at a single value):**
- **MODALITY axis** — `text` (default) / `vision-language` / `audio(LALM)` / `video` / `agent`. ← almost the entire catalog = text; refusal geometry outside text is barely studied.
- **ACCESS axis (threat model)** — `white-box` (have activations/weights) / `grey-box` (probe-guided, partial forward) / `black-box`. ← probe-as-attack (grey-box) is a separate branch.
- **STAGE axis (lifecycle)** — `pretraining` / `supervised-finetuning` / `rlhf-alignment` / `finetune-time` (harmful-finetuning attack/defense) / `inference-time`. ← almost everything = inference-time; "direction drift under finetune" is a separate branch.
- **LOCUS axis (unit of analysis, a ladder)** — `token → direction → cone-subspace → sparse-autoencoder-feature → neuron → circuit-head → mixture-of-experts-expert → weights`. ← refines MECH (which conflates "method" and "unit").
- **BEHAVIOR axis (concept)** — `refusal` / `harmfulness` / `over-refusal` / `sycophancy` / `deception` / `emergent-misalignment` / `truth` / `persona-traits`. ← previously implicit in the "other directions/circuits" cluster; we make it an explicit axis (the directions/cones apparatus generalizes to any BEHAVIOR).
- **POLARITY axis (failure type)** — `under-refusal` (jailbreak) ↔ `over-refusal` (excessive refusal). ← the whole map was about under-refusal; over-refusal has a SEPARATE geometry (P107).

**Axes from edge-discovery (consolidated in revision 25; no longer marked exotic):** these axes were proposed while building future-work edges, then run through a consolidation round that merged look-alikes (the four former boolean flags `temporal`/`training-dynamics`/`backdoor`/`identifiability` collapsed to two: `temporal`+`training-dynamics` -> one `DYNAMICS` axis; `identifiability` renamed `non-unique-direction`; `backdoor` dropped as redundant with the `RQ23` row that already carries its slice; `architecture` de-exoticised). See the playbook's axis-lifecycle consolidation step.
- **DYNAMICS** — `static` / `across-generation` / `across-training`. Whether refusal is studied as a fixed mechanism, as **evolving across decoding tokens** within one generation (anchor→onset→terminal-attenuation; jailbreak = "incomplete internal recovery" of the trajectory), or as the direction **emerging/drifting/relocating across training checkpoints** (adversarial-FT relocates the carrier rather than expanding dimensionality). Merges the former temporal + training-dynamics flags (P118/P119; P117/P120, OLMo).
- **ARCHITECTURE** — `dense` / `mixture-of-experts` / `state-space-model` / `diffusion-language-model`. Most interp tools transfer to Mamba/RWKV, but recurrent-state brings its own vulnerabilities; diffusion language models have their own refusal dynamics (P113/P114, P118).
- **non-unique-direction** (axis id IDENTIFIABILITY) — the direction is **not unique**: infinitely many directions give the same behavioral shift → a direct challenge to "counting mechanisms" (RQ7) and predict-control (RQ16).

Compute: S = a single 24GB card; M = 1×A100/H100; L = several GPUs/days.

> Machine-readable mirror of this legend (consumed by `scripts.field_map.parse_map`).
> The prose above stays authoritative for humans; the blocks below transcribe it.
> The `fieldmap-papers` block carries one row per (paper × tensor point) with the point's
> FULL coordinates; cells are DERIVED by grouping those rows, so there is no
> `fieldmap-cells` block. `parse_map --check` validates the blocks below.

```fieldmap-meta
id: refusal_geometry
structure_profile: current
low_cardinality_axes: CODE, MODELS, TRANSFER, ACCESS, POLARITY, DYNAMICS, ARCHITECTURE, IDENTIFIABILITY
title: Refusal directions, harm schemas, evasion and defense of probe/latent monitors
tldr: Where an LLM's refusal behavior lives, breaks, and is defended — refusal directions, jailbreaks, sparse-autoencoder features, probes and safety neurons.
as_of: 2026-06
multi_cell_expected: false
```

```fieldmap-about
Reads refusal as a geometric object inside the model, sliced by where the mechanism lives (token → direction → subspace → feature → neuron → circuit → weights) and the lens used to study it. Every axis is read per paper, so each cell is one coordinate point of the shared tensor; an authored empty point is a research gap.
```

P25	JailbreakLens	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.11114	Zeqing He et al.	2024	{"LOCUS": "circuit-head", "MECH": "attention", "RQ": "RQ3", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-generation"}	["circuit-head__attention"]	Although jailbreak attacks are prevalent, the understanding of their underlying mechanisms remains limited. P90	LLMs Encode Harmfulness and Refusal Separately	https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.11878		2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "harmfulness", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	Shows that harmfulness and refusal are encoded by separate causal directions, that jailbreaks suppress the refusal signal without changing the model's internal belief that a prompt is harmful, and that a harmfulness-based latent guard matches Llama-Guard-3-8B while resisting fine-tuning attacks.	**harmfulness direction ≠ refusal direction** (causal); jailbreak suppresses the refusal signal WITHOUT changing the "belief" in harmfulness; Latent Guard ≈ Llama-Guard-3-8B, robust to finetune-attack

P32	Understanding Refusal in Language Models with SAEs	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.23556	Wei Jie Yeo et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "sparse-autoencoder-feature", "MECH": "sparse-autoencoders", "RQ": "RQ9", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["sparse-autoencoder-feature__sparse-autoencoders", "code:released (refusal_sae)"]	In this work, we conduct a mechanistic study of refusal in instruction-tuned LLMs using sparse autoencoders to identify latent features that causally mediate refusal behaviors. P35	Towards Universality: Mechanistic Similarity Across Architectures	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.06672	Junxuan Wang et al.	2024	{"LOCUS": "sparse-autoencoder-feature", "MECH": "sparse-autoencoders", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "partial", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "state-space-model", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["sparse-autoencoder-feature__sparse-autoencoders", "code:partial"]	In this work, we investigate two mainstream architectures for language modeling, namely Transformers and Mambas, to explore the extent of their mechanistic similarity. P36	Universal Sparse Autoencoders (USAE)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.03714	Harrish Thasarathan et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "sparse-autoencoder-feature", "MECH": "sparse-autoencoders", "RQ": "RQ9", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["sparse-autoencoder-feature__sparse-autoencoders"]	Unlike existing concept-based interpretability methods, which focus on a single model, USAEs jointly learn a universal concept space that can reconstruct and interpret the internal activations of multiple models at once. P88	Persona Vectors: Monitoring & Controlling Traits	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.21509	Runjin Chen et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "persona-traits", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["direction__directions", "code:released (safety-research/persona_vectors)"]	While the Assistant is typically trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest, it sometimes deviates from these ideals. In this paper, we identify directions in the model's activation space-persona vectors-underlying several traits, such as evil, sycophancy, and propensity to hallucinate.	**trait directions** (evil/sycophancy/hallucination) — auto extraction pipeline, drift monitoring, inference/preventative steering, screening of training data (r=0.76–0.97)
P89	Sycophancy as a Persona-Level Property	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.21006	Ishaan Kelkar et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "sycophancy", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	The standard mitigation, Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), derives a steering direction from labelled pairs of sycophantic and honest responses. This study evaluates whether off-the-shelf persona steering vectors, originally developed for general role-playing and not trained on sycophancy data, can serve as an alternative.	off-the-shelf persona vectors ≈ targeted CAA without sycophancy supervision; **the polarity of the role↔CAA vector is model-specific** (Gemma + / Qwen −) ← transfer of NON-refusal directions is also model-specific in sign
P115	What Can We Actually Steer? (multi-behavior)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.18284	Tetiana Bas et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "BEHAVIOR=multi"]	Activation steering offers a promising approach for LLMs' behavioral control. We focus on the question of how steering effectiveness varies across different behavior types and whether the nature of target behaviors can predict steering success.	**50 behaviors** in 5 categories — persona archetypes (vegan/pirate/…), **Big Five** traits, misalignment (sycophancy/deception/manipulation/hallucination/dark-triad), style/format cues, public figures; inverted-U by steering strength; misalignment is the most steerable (+60 trait), public figures the worst ← a catalog of "what is already steerable" (related: "Can LLMs Lie?" [pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.03518), Curveball-steering [pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.09313))
P137	A Low-Rank Subspace Analysis of LLM Interventions	https://arxiv.org/html/2606.14388v1	Sharma et al	2026	{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ24", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "yes", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["cone-subspace__directions", "LOCUS=cone-subspace", "non-unique-direction"]	Demonstrates that LLM behaviors such as refusal, jailbreaking, and sycophancy are encoded as low-rank subspaces rather than single directions, characterizes their overlap using principal angles and a shared refuse-comply decision subspace, and shows that cross-behavior steering effects are asymmetric across models from 7B to 70B.	behaviors = **low-rank subspaces** (not 1D); overlap via principal angles + a **decision subspace** (refuse↔comply); cross-behavior effects are **asymmetric** (upstream controllers); refusal/jailbreak/sycophancy on 7B–70B ← **occupies the "cones/subspaces for NON-refusal" niche**, but readout geometry, not a causal count
P138	Local Linearity Enables Activation Steering via A-LQR	https://arxiv.org/html/2604.19018v1	Skifstad et al	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	Introduces a closed-loop activation steering method that combines layer-wise Jacobians with a linear-quadratic regulator to reliably modulate concepts such as toxicity, truthfulness, and refusal, with formal bounds on tracking error and state-of-the-art control over arbitrary concepts.	closed-loop steering via layer-wise Jacobians + LQR; SOTA modulation of **toxicity/truthfulness/refusal** and arbitrary concepts; formal bounds on tracking error
P139	Steering Code LLMs with Activation Directions	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.23629	Md Mahbubur Rahman et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "code", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=code"]	We investigate whether these preferences are encoded as approximately linear directions in activation space that can be manipulated at inference time. P140	Attention-Guided Concept Vector Extraction	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.00333	Parmida Davarmanesh et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	Yet, existing steering methods are remarkably brittle, with seemingly non-steerable concepts becoming completely steerable based on subtle algorithmic choices in how concept-related features are extracted. In this work, we introduce an attention-guided steering framework that overcomes three core challenges associated with steering: (1) automatic selection of relevant token embeddings for …	attention-guided extraction of concept vectors; a benchmark of **512 semantic concepts** (languages, affect, politics, **refusal**); ~2× successful steerings vs SOTA; up to 70B
P153	Sycophancy Is Not One Thing (causal separation)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.21305	Daniel Vennemeyer et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "sycophancy", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "BEHAVIOR=sycophancy"]	We decompose sycophancy into sycophantic agreement and sycophantic praise, contrasting both with genuine agreement. Using difference-in-means directions, activation additions, and subspace geometry across multiple models and datasets, we show that: (1) the three behaviors are encoded along distinct linear directions in latent space; (2) each behavior can be independently amplified or suppressed …	**causal separation** of 3 sycophancy behaviors (agreement/praise/genuine) into distinct linear directions; each can be **independently amplified/suppressed** without affecting the others (activation additions); the structure is **consistent across families/scales** ← causal count + independence for non-refusal, but the authors admit: **only linear, no nonlinear/RepInd**
P154	Shared Sycophancy-Lying Circuit	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.19117	Manav Pandey	2026	{"LOCUS": "circuit-head", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "sycophancy", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "BEHAVIOR=sycophancy/deception"]	We show the latter. P155	Sycophancy Direction in Attention Heads (deference resistance)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.16644	Rifo Genadi et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "circuit-head", "MECH": "attention", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "sycophancy", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["circuit-head__attention", "BEHAVIOR=sycophancy"]	Motivated by the linear representation hypothesis, we train linear probes across the residual stream, multilayer perceptron (MLP), and attention layers to analyze where these signals emerge. Although separability appears in the residual stream and MLPs, steering using these probes is most effective in a sparse subset of middle-layer attention heads.	the correct→incorrect sycophancy signal is concentrated in **attention heads** (MLP/residual more diffuse); partially overlaps the truthful direction, but is **independently modulable**; **linear-probe steering > nonlinear** ← a non-refusal direction, overlap with truth; nonlinear structure not exhausted
P96	Representation Without Control (realization effect)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25151	Ciarán Walsh et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ16", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	We study this question through the realization effect, a well-characterized finding in behavioral economics in which risk-taking differs systematically after paper versus realized gains and losses. P97	Linear Probes Detect Task Format, Not Reasoning Mode	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02907	Subramanyam Sahoo et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ4", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	We test this by probing Qwen3-14B on three benchmarks spanning the classical trichotomy: LogiQA 2.0 (deductive), ARC-Challenge (inductive), and $α$NLI (abductive). P98	One Agency Axis, Not Five Traits (SAE-probe steering, 35B MoE)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.16335	Jia Qing Yap	2026	{"LOCUS": "sparse-autoencoder-feature", "MECH": "sparse-autoencoders", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "agent", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "mixture-of-experts", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "yes", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["sparse-autoencoder-feature__sparse-autoencoders"]	Our method trains linear probes on SAE latent activations, then projects the probe weights back through the SAE decoder to obtain continuous steering vectors in the model's native activation space. P99	Where Do Reasoning Models Refuse?	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.03167	Kureha Yamaguchi et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ15", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "partial", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-generation"}	["direction__directions"]	Reasoning models, by contrast, produce extended chains of thought before their final output, raising a natural question: where in this process does the decision to refuse occur? P150	Activation Consistency Training (ACT) for reasoning models	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28467	Avidan Shah et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ15", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions"]	We study consistency training, a family of fine-tuning objectives that enforce identical behavior on clean prompts and adversarial rewrites, and evaluate its two main variants, output-level (BCT) and activation-level (ACT), across five reasoning models. P151	H-CoT: Hijacking the Chain-of-Thought Safety Reasoning	https://www.researchgate.net/publication/389130378	Kuo et al	2025	{"LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "RQ": "RQ10", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["token__behavior"]	Introduces H-CoT, a universal and transferable prompt-level attack that hijacks the safety chain-of-thought of large reasoning models (o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1, Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking), collapsing their refusal rate from about 98% to under 2%.	a **universal transferable** attack on LRMs (o1/o3, R1, Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking) — chain-of-thought substitution, refusal 98%→<2% ← a prompt-level chain-of-thought hijack that transfers across reasoning models (NOT transfer of an activation-level evasion — open)
P101	Safety Geometry Collapse in Multimodal LLMs	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18104	Jiahe Guo et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language"]	We study this gap from a representation-geometric perspective by analyzing a text-aligned refusal direction and a modality-induced drift direction. P102	Do LLMs and VLMs Share Neurons for Inference?	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.19058	Chenhang Cui et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "neuron", "MECH": "neurons", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "yes", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["neuron__neurons", "code:released", "MODALITY=vision-language"]	Motivated by their shared transformer architectures, we investigate whether the two model families rely on common internal computation for such inference. P106	Indirect Prompt Injection in the Wild	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.27202	Soheil Khodayari et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "agent", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["token__behavior", "MODALITY=agent"]	This enables site owners, contributors, and adversaries to embed instructions directly in web resources, i.e., indirect prompt injections. P112	SARSteer: Safe-Ablated Refusal Steering (audio LALM)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.17633	Weilin Lin et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "audio", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "code:released (linweiii/SARSteer)", "MODALITY=audio"]	However, recent studies show that audio inputs can more easily elicit harmful responses than text, exposing new risks toward deployment. P123	VLM Jailbreaks via Jailbreak-Related Representation Shift (JRS-Rem)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.17372	Zhihua Wei et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language"]	Even when text prompts contain explicit harmful intent, adding an image can substantially increase jailbreak success rates. In this paper, we observe that VLMs can clearly distinguish benign inputs from harmful ones in their representation space.	**the image shifts the representation toward a separate jailbreak state** (separable from benign and refusal); a **jailbreak direction** = refuse→jailbreak; JRS-Rem removes the shift at inference ← a refusal direction exists in VLMs
P124	NullSteer: Null-space Projected Steering (VLM defense)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.22094	Xingyu Zhu et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language"]	Recent activation steering methods inject directional vectors into model activations during inference to induce refusal behaviors and have demonstrated effectiveness. However, a steering vector may both enhance refusal ability and cause over-refusal, thereby degrading model performance on benign inputs.	the refusal direction in a **subspace orthogonal to benign** (zero perturbation on benign) → safety without over-refusal (MiniGPT-4 −15% ASR) ← porting the null-space/LEACE idea into VLMs
P125	JailBound: Internal Safety Boundary of VLM	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.19610	Jiaxin Song et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "yes", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language", "ACCESS=white-box→black-box"]	However, lacking well-defined attack objectives, existing jailbreak methods often struggle with gradient-based strategies prone to local optima and lacking precise directional guidance, and typically decouple visual and textual modalities, thereby limiting their effectiveness by neglecting crucial cross-modal interactions. Inspired by the Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK) framework, we posit that …	probing the safety boundary by layer (100% acc), joint image+text attack across the boundary; white-box ASR 94% (LLaMA-3.2), transfer to GPT-4o 75%
P152	Encoding-Behavior Dissociation in VLMs	https://openreview.net/forum?id=GGFjPyO3mZ		2026	{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "no", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["cone-subspace__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language", "LOCUS=cone-subspace"]	Provides the first measurement of the dimensionality of the safety representation in vision-language models, showing that encoding harmful-versus-benign inputs requires about fifty PCA components (versus one in text LLMs) and is robust to iterative ablation, while behavior still reduces to a single threshold and adversarial evasions transfer weakly across models (below 11%).	**the first measurement of the DIMENSIONALITY of the safety representation in VLMs** — encoding harmful↔benign requires **~50 PCA components** (vs 1 in text-LLMs), the signal spans all positions/heads, **robust to iterative ablation** (vs Gemma-IT collapse after ~11); BUT behaviorally — a single **DIM threshold** (benign refusal 52→98%); per-image/universal PGD evasion 98.4%/96.9% (ε=8/255), **cross-model transfer weak ≤11%** ← dimensionality outside text (encoding-dim, not a RepInd cone) + evasion transfers weakly (cross-model ≤11%)
P131	Jailbreaking MLLMs using Multi-Clip Video (MCV)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02111	Choongwon Kang et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "video", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=video"]	Prior jailbreak studies have shown that safety alignment in MLLMs can be bypassed through visual inputs, yet it remains unclear which properties of video inputs induce this vulnerability. To address this gap, we introduce Multi-Clip Video (MCV) SafetyBench, a dataset of 2,920 videos designed to evaluate how the diversity of video inputs affects the vulnerability of MLLMs.	video is **more dangerous than images**; the more diverse the clips, the more the model **internally represents the input as LESS harmful** → frame-filter defense (related: VSE-Eval benchmark [pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.16643), SPTV safety-proximal frames [link](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=qVMtipJxgE)) ← closes the previously empty MODALITY=video cell
P135	OmniSteer: Omni-Safety under Cross-Modality Conflict	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.10161	Kun Wang et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "vision-language", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=vision-language/VLM (cross-modal)"]	However, a systematic understanding of vulnerabilities in omni-modal interactions remains lacking. To bridge this gap, we establish a modality-semantics decoupling principle and construct the AdvBench-Omni dataset, which reveals a significant vulnerability in OLLMs.	a **unified cross-modal "golden" refusal vector via SVD** (text/audio/image/video), layer-wise adaptive steering by adapters; RSR 69.9→91.2% ← the closest thing to a refusal SUBSPACE in audio + transfer across modalities
P136	Acoustic Interference (AIA, audio mechanism)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18168	Yanyun Wang et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ18", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "audio", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "MODALITY=audio"]	Existing jailbreak paradigms predominantly treat audio as a carrier for malicious payloads, relying on semantic optimization, acoustic parameter control, or additive perturbation to embed harmful content into the audio signal. In this work, we challenge this necessity and propose a new paradigm in which the role of audio shifts from content injection to safety alignment interference.	**benign-by-content audio with paralinguistics = a universal trigger**; the mechanism — **inference path drift** that steers the internal representation **out of the safety subspace** (interpretable ALS patterns) ← an audio analog of "shifting out of the cone"
P107	Over-Refusal and Representation Subspaces	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.27518	Utsav Maskey et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ17", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "over-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["cone-subspace__directions", "POLARITY=over-refusal"]	A natural approach is to ablate the global refusal direction, steering the hidden-state vectors away or towards the harmful-refusal examples, but this corrects over-refusal only incidentally while disrupting the broader refusal mechanism. In this work, we analyse the representational geometry of both refusal types to understand why this happens.	harmful refusal = a task-agnostic single direction (early-mid layers); **over-refusal = a task-dependent, higher-dimensional subspace** within task-identity clusters → global ablation misses, a task-conditioned steering is needed (LLaMA-3.1-8B)
P108	Beyond Over-Refusal (MS-XSB diagnostics)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.08158	Shuzhou Yuan et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "RQ": "RQ17", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "over-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["token__behavior", "POLARITY=over-refusal"]	We address this challenge by introducing two comprehensive benchmarks: the Exaggerated Safety Benchmark (XSB) for single-turn prompts, annotated with "Focus" keywords that identify refusal-inducing triggers, and the Multi-turn Scenario-based Exaggerated Safety Benchmark (MS-XSB), which systematically evaluates refusal calibration in realistic, context-rich dialog settings. Our benchmarks reveal …	a scenario-based multi-turn over-refusal benchmark + post-hoc mitigation (ignore-word/rephrase/attention-steer) without weight access
P109	Safety Anchor: Defending Harmful Fine-tuning (Geometric Bottleneck)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05995	Guoxin Lu et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ6", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__training", "STAGE=finetune-time"]	While existing defenses impose constraints on parameters, gradients, or internal representations, we observe that they can be effectively circumvented under persistent HFT. Our analysis traces this failure to the inherent redundancy of the high-dimensional parameter space: attackers exploit optimization trajectories that are orthogonal to defense constraints to restore harmful capabilities while …	harmful-finetuning exploits **parameter redundancy**; the defense ports to the unembedding "bottleneck" (SBR), a single safety-anchor holds safety; refusal⊥benign-reasoning
P110	Refusal in Compressed Models (AIRD)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.04215	Vishnu Kabir Chhabra et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ19", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "compress", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["direction__directions", "STAGE=compress"]	While extensive research has explored model compression through the lens of safety, findings suggest that safety-aligned models often lose elements of trustworthiness post-compression. Simultaneously, the field of mechanistic interpretability has gained traction, with notable discoveries, such as the identification of a single direction in the residual stream mediating refusal behaviors across …	**pruning SHIFTS the source of the refusal direction (loses safety), quantization PRESERVES it**; AIRD restores it ← transfer along the compression axis
P111	Analysing the Safety Pitfalls of Steering Vectors	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.24543	Yuxiao Li et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ19", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__training"]	While its inherent brittleness and unreliability are well-documented, its safety implications remain underexplored. In this work, we present a systematic safety audit of steering vectors obtained with Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a widely used steering approach, under a unified evaluation protocol.	CAA steering vectors overlap with the refusal direction (bimodal cosine) → ±50% ASR; a mechanistic explanation of "steering externalities" (related to P21)
P116	RefusalGuard: Geometry-Preserving Fine-Tuning	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01913	Sadia Asif et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ19", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["cone-subspace__training", "STAGE=finetune-time"]	While prior work has shown that safety-relevant features are encoded in structured representations within the model's activation space, how these representations change during fine-tuning and why alignment degrades remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the representation-level mechanisms underlying alignment degradation.	finetune degrades safety via **3 effects** — (i) directional drift, (ii) geometric distortion, (iii) interference of task↔safety updates; the defense preserves the geometry
P117	Anchoring Refusal Direction (ProCon, drift under IFT)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.06795	Yanrui Du et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ19", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["direction__training", "STAGE=finetune-time"]	However, prior studies have shown that IFT can significantly compromise LLMs' safety, particularly their ability to refuse malicious instructions, raising significant concerns. P126	Tracing Persona Vectors Through Pretraining	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13329	Viktor Moskvoretskii et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ14", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "pretraining", "BEHAVIOR": "persona-traits", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["direction__training", "STAGE=pretraining"]	Recent work has shown that traits such as evil or sycophancy correspond to linear directions in the internal activations, the so-called persona vectors. P134	DeepRefusal: Probabilistically Ablating Refusal Direction	https://aclanthology.org/2025.findings-emnlp.956.pdf		2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ6", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-generation"}	["direction__training", "STAGE=finetune-time+DYNAMICS=across-generation", "code:released (YuanBoXie/DeepRefusal)"]	Proposes DeepRefusal, a fine-tuning defense that probabilistically ablates the refusal direction across layers and token depths so the model learns to rebuild refusal from a jailbroken state at any position, reducing attack success rate by about 95%.	the defense **randomly ablates the refusal direction across layers AND token depths** during FT → the model learns to **rebuild refusal from a jailbreak state** at any depth; −95% ASR ← confirms that safety is distributed over token-depth (echo of P133/shallow-safety P30)
P113	SAFEx: Safety-Critical Experts in MoE	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.17368	Zhenglin Lai et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "mixture-of-experts-expert", "MECH": "neurons", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "mixture-of-experts", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["mixture-of-experts-expert__neurons", "LOCUS=mixture-of-experts-expert"]	In this work, the MoE-specific safety risk of positional vulnerability-that safety-aligned behaviors rely on specific expert modules-is formalized and systematically analyzed. P114	Understanding Safety-Sensitive Expert Behavior (RASET)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29708	Zhibo Zhang et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "mixture-of-experts-expert", "MECH": "neurons", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "mixture-of-experts", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["mixture-of-experts-expert__neurons", "LOCUS=mixture-of-experts-expert"]	A common intuition is that safety behavior may be controlled by routing harmful requests to distinct refusal-oriented experts. In this work, we provide empirical evidence for a different picture: routing patterns in aligned MoE LLMs are largely topic-driven, while safety behavior can be altered with little change to the model's intrinsic routing path.	is refusal via **routing to safety experts** OR within topic experts? P120	Dynamic Adversarial FT Reorganizes Refusal Geometry	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.27019	Wenhao Lan et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ19", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-training"}	["direction__training", "DYNAMICS=across-training"]	We study a 7B backbone under supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Robust Refusal Dynamic Defense (R2D2), aligning HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest evaluations with five-anchor geometry measurements, causal interventions, and sparse adaptive stress tests. P128	DiJA: The Devil behind the mask (dLLM jailbreak)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.11097	Zichen Wen et al.	2025	{"LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "diffusion-language-model", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["token__behavior", "ARCHITECTURE=diffusion-language-model", "code:released"]	However, despite strong performance in code generation and text infilling, we identify a fundamental safety concern: existing alignment mechanisms fail to safeguard dLLMs against context-aware, masked-input adversarial prompts, exposing novel vulnerabilities. To this end, we present DIJA, the first systematic study and jailbreak attack framework that exploits unique safety weaknesses of dLLMs.	the first systematic jailbreak of diffusion language model — interleaved mask-text exploits **bidirectionality + parallel decoding** (no dynamic filtering); up to 100% ASR (Dream/LLaDA)
P129	TrajHijack: Re-Mask and Redirect (dLLM)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.08557	Arth Singh	2026	{"LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "diffusion-language-model", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "across-generation"}	["token__behavior", "ARCHITECTURE=diffusion-language-model+DYNAMICS=across-generation"]	We show that violating this assumption, by re-masking committed refusal tokens and injecting a short affirmative prefix, achieves 74-82% ASR on HarmBench across all three publicly available safety-tuned dLLMs, rising to 92-98% with a generic 8-token compliance prefix. P132	Refusal in LLMs is an Affine Function (ACE on RWKV/SSM)	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.09003	Thomas Marshall et al.	2024	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "state-space-model", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "ARCHITECTURE=state-space-model"]	We begin with an affine decomposition of model activation vectors and show that prior methods for steering model behavior correspond to subsets of terms of this decomposition. P121	Sleeper Agents	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.05566	Evan Hubinger et al.	2024	{"LOCUS": "weights", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ23", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "deception", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["weights__training"]	If an AI system learned such a deceptive strategy, could we detect it and remove it using current state-of-the-art safety training techniques? To study this question, we construct proof-of-concept examples of deceptive behavior in large language models (LLMs).	backdoors **survive safety-training** (RL/SFT/adversarial); adversarial-training **hides** rather than removes; defection is **linearly detected** (>99% AUROC defection-probe) ← a probe on conditional behavior
P122	Triggers Hijack Language Circuits	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.10382	Théo Lasnier et al.	2026	{"LOCUS": "circuit-head", "MECH": "attention", "RQ": "RQ23", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "deception", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["circuit-head__attention"]	We present the first mechanistic analysis of language-switching backdoors, studying the GAPperon model family (1B, 8B, 24B parameters) which contains triggers injected during pretraining that cause output language switching. Using activation patching, we localize trigger formation to early layers (7.5-25% of model depth) and identify which attention heads process trigger information.	triggers **co-opt existing circuits** (overlap with language-heads) rather than build separate ones; form early (7.5–25% of depth) — a mechanistic analysis of the backdoor
P130	Provably Extracting Features from a General Superposition	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.15987	Allen Liu	2025	{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ24", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "yes", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	["direction__directions", "non-unique-direction"]	This is the foundational hypothesis behind a vast body of work on interpretability. A key challenge toward extracting interpretable features, however, is that they exist in superposition.	in superposition, features are **identifiable ⇔ the response functions are NONlinear**; linearity is the only obstacle ← a theoretical basis for counting: "counting mechanisms" is correct only with a nonlinear criterion (= RepInd P18, nonlinear refusal P83); related to the spectral theory of superposition [pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.02224)
P156	Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety, Even When Users Do Not Intend To!	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.03693	Xiangyu Qi, Yi Zeng, Tinghao Xie, Pin-Yu Chen, Ruoxi Jia, Prateek Mittal	2023	{"RQ": "RQ12", "LOCUS": "weights", "MECH": "training", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "DYNAMICS": "across-training", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["weights__training", "code:released", "landmark"]	Fine-tuning an aligned model on as few as 10 adversarial (or even benign) examples strips its safety alignment, showing alignment is shallow at the weight level.	Few-shot fine-tuning undoes safety alignment.
P157	BeaverTails: Towards Improved Safety Alignment of LLM via a Human-Preference Dataset	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.04657	Jiaming Ji, Mickel Liu, Juntao Dai, Xuehai Pan, Chi Zhang, Ce Bian	2023	{"RQ": "RQ13", "LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "rlhf-alignment", "BEHAVIOR": "harmfulness", "POLARITY": "neutral", "DYNAMICS": "static", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["token__behavior", "code:released"]	BeaverTails separates helpfulness and harmlessness annotations across 330k QA pairs, a resource for safety RLHF and content moderation. P186	WildTeaming at Scale: From In-the-Wild Jailbreaks to (Adversarially) Safer Language Models	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.18510	Liwei Jiang, Kavel Rao, Seungju Han, Allyson Ettinger, Faeze Brahman, Sachin Kumar	2024	{"RQ": "RQ13", "LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "DYNAMICS": "static", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["token__behavior", "code:released"]	WildTeaming mines 5.7k in-the-wild jailbreak-tactic clusters and releases WildJailbreak (262k pairs), studying the safety/over-refusal trade-off in training. P187	Fundamental Limitations of Alignment in Large Language Models	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.11082	Yotam Wolf, Noam Wies, Oshri Avnery, Yoav Levine, Amnon Shashua	2023	{"RQ": "RQ12", "LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "black-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "DYNAMICS": "static", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["token__behavior", "landmark"]	Behavior Expectation Bounds prove any behavior with finite probability can be triggered by a long-enough prompt, so alignment that only attenuates is not adversarially safe. P188	LoRA Fine-tuning Efficiently Undoes Safety Training in Llama 2-Chat 70B	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.20624	Simon Lermen, Charlie Rogers-Smith, Jeffrey Ladish	2023	{"RQ": "RQ12", "LOCUS": "weights", "MECH": "training", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "finetune-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "DYNAMICS": "across-training", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["weights__training", "code:released"]	LoRA fine-tuning under $200 on one GPU removes Llama-2-Chat safety (refusal ~1%) while keeping capabilities, across 7B/13B/70B and Mixtral. Robust prompt-optimization defense.
P192	RAIN: Your Language Models Can Align Themselves without Finetuning	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.07124	Yuhui Li, Fangyun Wei, Jinjing Zhao, Chao Zhang, Hongyang Zhang	2023	{"RQ": "RQ6", "LOCUS": "token", "MECH": "behavior", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "neutral", "DYNAMICS": "static", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["token__behavior", "code:released"]	RAIN adds self-evaluation and rewind to let a frozen LLM self-align at inference (harmlessness 82%->97%) with no training or extra data. P537	Extracting Unlearned Information from LLMs with Activation Steering	https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.02631	Atakan Seyitoğlu, Aleksei Kuvshinov, Leo Schwinn, Stephan Günnemann	2024	{"RQ": "RQ10", "LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "directions", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "truth", "POLARITY": "neutral", "DYNAMICS": "static", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no"}	["direction__directions", "code:not-released"]	An unintended consequence of the vast pretraining of Large Language Models (LLMs) is the verbatim memorization of fragments of their training data, which may contain sensitive or copyrighted information. {"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ5", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Turn the currently empirical, white-box layer-susceptibility result into a standardized, layer-targeted activation-steering method (F24) that bypasses the most susceptible layers without fine-tuning, and test it for certified or provable robustness (F20) against the adaptive and obfuscation attacks whose resistance is so far unproven.

{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ4", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	The linear case is solved but the nonlinear one looks intractable because refusal may encode nonlinearly: test directly whether refusal is erasable linearly or whether nonlinear methods are required (F2), bounding where linear ablation stops working.
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ7", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Test whether the overcomplete basis of refusal directions is cross-family and whether its dimensionality drops with scale: measure transfer of the direction and geometry across model families, scales and architectures (F1).
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ1", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Pin down the read-versus-steer gap where the refusal signal reads and transfers but steering fails to move behavior (F34): run ablations crossed with paraphrase, scale and temperature under a rigorously powered evaluation pipeline with a justified sample size (F25), separating a genuine causal steering failure from underpowered or confounded measurement.
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ4", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Probe accuracy collapses from 100% to chance after residualization: re-test the claim on format-controlled benchmarks that guard against contamination and overfitting (F26), so the signal reflects the behavior rather than spurious format cues.
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ22", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "single", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "multi", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "mixture-of-experts", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "yes", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Explain why orthogonal refusal directions show opposite causal effectiveness in a mixture-of-experts model: run interventional tests that establish which direction is causally responsible for the safety behavior rather than merely correlated (F4).
{"LOCUS": "direction", "MECH": "training", "RQ": "RQ8", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "yes", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "neutral", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Show that weight-baked guardrails break under distillation: measure how much safety a student inherits from a guarded teacher and develop training that is robust to this finetuning- and distillation-induced safety degradation (F11).
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ8", "CODE": "released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Test transfer of the refusal intervention to non-safety domains, studying cross-domain transfer and model merging of the direction (F33) to see whether it generalizes beyond safety tasks.
{"LOCUS": "cone-subspace", "MECH": "directions", "RQ": "RQ7", "CODE": "not-released", "MODELS": "multi", "TRANSFER": "not-tested", "MODALITY": "text", "ACCESS": "white-box", "STAGE": "inference-time", "BEHAVIOR": "refusal", "POLARITY": "under-refusal", "ARCHITECTURE": "dense", "IDENTIFIABILITY": "no", "DYNAMICS": "static"}	Develop fine-grained, per-layer modulation of the refusal cone, standardizing and layer-targeting the activation-steering method (F24) so the strength of refusal can be tuned reliably at the most effective layers.
